  • Background

GitHub

Introduction

Production–regeneration systems describe processes that must alternate between a productive phase and a recovery phase. During production, the system generates an output (for example filtered water), but this phase also progressively degrades an internal state (such as fouling or resistance). The regeneration phase does the opposite: it restores the system’s internal condition, but does not directly produce useful output.

Such problem can be formulated as an optimal control problem, and the main goal of this package is to provide solutions of such problem.

Problem statement

Let consider the following tree dimentional dynamic in production and regeneration mode

\[\begin{array}{ccc} \dot c(t) = l_p(x(t)) & \text{and} & \dot c(t) = l_r(x(t)), \\[0.5em] \dot x(t) = f_p(x(t)) & \text{and} & \dot x(t) = f_r(x(t)), \\[0.5em] \dot y(t) = g_p(x(t)) & \text{and} & \dot y(t) = g_r(x(t)), \end{array}\]

where :

  • $c$ is the cost,
  • $x$ corresponds to the internal state,
  • $y$ is a state linked to a target to reach (or a budget constraint), denoted $T$.

Denoting the control $u \in [-1, 1]$, where $u = +1$ denotes production mode and $u = -1$ denotes regeneration mode, we look at the following optimal control problem

\[\left\{ \begin {array}{ll} \displaystyle \min_{x,y,t_f} \int_{t_0}^{t_f} \frac{1 + u(t)}{2} l_p(x(t)) + \frac{1-u(t)}{2} l_r(x(t)) \, \mathrm dt, & t \in [t_0, t_f] \ \mathrm{a.e.}, \\[1em] \displaystyle \mathrm{s.t.} \ \dot x(t) = \frac{1 + u(t)}{2} f_p(x(t)) + \frac{1-u(t)}{2} f_r(x(t)) \, \mathrm dt, & t \in [t_0, t_f] \ \mathrm{a.e.}, \\[1em] \displaystyle \phantom{\mathrm{s.t.} \ } \dot y(t) = \frac{1 + u(t)}{2} g_p(x(t)) + \frac{1-u(t)}{2} g_r(x(t)) \, \mathrm dt, & t \in [t_0, t_f] \ \mathrm{a.e.}, \\[1em] \phantom{\mathrm{s.t.} \ } u(t) \in [-1, 1], & t \in [t_0, t_f], \\[1em] \phantom{\mathrm{s.t.} \ } x(t_0) = x_0, \quad y(t_0) = y_0, \quad y(t_f) = T, \end{array} \right.\]

where $t_0 \in \mathbb R$, $x_0 > 0$, $y_0 > 0$ and $T > 0$ are provided.

Main theorical results

Thanks to theorical developement done in Dutto et al., 2026, all the possible structure of solutions are given in the following theorem

Theorem

Under some classical hypothesis, and denoting

  • $\sigma_-$ an arc associated to $u = -1$,
  • $\sigma_+$ an arc associated to $u = +1$,
  • $\sigma_s$ an arc associated to $u = u_s$,

the structure of an optimal solution can only be one of

\[\sigma_+, \ \sigma_-\sigma_+, \ \sigma_s\sigma_+, \ \sigma_-\sigma_s\sigma_+ ~ \text{or} ~ \sigma_+\sigma_s\sigma_+.\]
